results.possibleIdentity: Howard Gutstadt (also known as Howie Gutstadt) is a pioneering video artist, media activist, and technologist who was a central figure in the early New York video art scene of the late 1960s and 1970s. In 1970, he co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) alongside Ken Marsh, and in 1972, he co-founded its successor, Survival Arts Media (SAM). Gutstadt utilized portable video technology, specifically the Sony PortaPak, for community activism, health education, and artistic expression. He is widely recognized for his 1971 archival video footage of teenagers at Camp Jened, which served as the foundational historical material for the 2020 Academy Award-nominated documentary 'Crip Camp.' Gutstadt was part of the early movement of artists exploring the intersection of art and science, frequently associated with the New York State Council on the Arts (NYSCA) initiatives and the broader context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was instrumental in organizing the 1972-1973 'Public Access Celebration' to promote cable television access for independent artists and collaborated with pioneers like Bill Etra on the development of early video synthesizers. In 1978, he moved to Berkeley, California, where he established Digital Image Productions and later worked with technology companies such as Autodesk and Diaquest to develop digital software and hardware tools for the arts. His work has been exhibited at SIGGRAPH (1982, 1985) and is preserved in the NYU Special Collections Library.

results.possibleIdentity: Juan O'Gorman (1905–1982) was a prominent Mexican architect and painter active through the 1960s and 1970s. He is noted for his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), specifically through his participation in the 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. This exhibition was organized by E.A.T. and showcased collaborative projects between artists and engineers. O'Gorman's work in this period involved shifts toward experimental forms, including what he termed 'Invisible Architecture.' He is famous for his mosaic murals, such as those on the Central Library of the University of Mexico.

R. C. Gorman (Rudolph Carl Gorman, 1931–2005) was a celebrated Navajo artist active during the late 1960s and 1970s. Known as the 'Picasso of American Indian artists,' his work transitioned from abstract Navajo rug designs in the 1960s to his iconic stylized depictions of Navajo women in the 1970s. There is evidence of his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in historical auction records and through the organization's projects involving the Navajo Nation in the early 1970s, such as 'Projects Outside,' which aimed to bring communication technology to Native American reservations.

Gregory Gorman (born 1949), commonly known as Greg Gorman, is an American photographer who began his career in the late 1960s documenting rock concerts (his first professional shoot was of Jimi Hendrix in 1968). Throughout the 1970s and beyond, he became one of the most recognized celebrity portrait photographers in Los Angeles. While primarily a photographer rather than a typical 'art and technology' collaborator, he was active in the relevant period and his initials 'G. Gorman' match the historical artist identity. He recently published a retrospective book titled 'It's Not About Me.'

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Newman (also known as Robert Bruce Newman) was an artist, poet, and critic active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He served as the director of the Spectrum Gallery (a cooperative in Manhattan) starting in 1966, where he famously gave artist Faith Ringgold her first solo exhibition in 1967. In April 1969, he founded the alternative space 'Gain Ground' on the Upper West Side, which served as a forum for verbal and visual interchange and showcased works by experimental artists like Vito Acconci and Dan Graham. Newman's own artistic practice involved 'concrete poetry' and a strong interest in advanced technology. He produced 'experiential events' such as 'Live Photography/Rooms with Electric Mirrors' and 'Power Throne' (1968), which utilized mirrored reflections and heart sounds. In 1970, he proposed a project to upgrade the lighting system of the Statue of Liberty's torch using advanced technology, demonstrating his commitment to the intersection of art and engineering. He later taught courses in art-and-technology at the University of Colorado.

Thelma R. Newman was an artist, educator, and author who was a pioneer in the use of plastics as a fine art medium during the 1960s and 1970s. She is the author of several influential books, including 'Plastics as an Art Form' (1964) and 'Plastics as Design Form' (1972). Her own sculpture, such as 'Surrogate Mother' (ca. 1961), explored the translucency and malleability of synthetic materials like Lucite and polyester resin. She is cited in the bibliography of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and collaborated with the organization to provide technical information for artists regarding new synthetic materials. Her work and writings were instrumental in the 'silent revolution' of chemical and industrial materials being adopted by visual artists during the postwar period.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert O'Brien (born 1939) is a British-born visual artist and art educator who has been based in Hong Kong since 1976. He graduated from Central Saint Martins (then the London Institute) in 1961 with a degree in Theatre Design. His career as an artist began in 1964, and he worked as a full-time artist in London and Munich between 1964 and 1975. This period aligns with the height of activity for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which was founded in 1966 and initially grew out of the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' project. O'Brien's background in theatre design and his presence in the London and Munich art scenes during the late 1960s and early 1970s place him within the collaborative milieu encouraged by E.A.T. His work has focused on the natural world, conceptual mapping, and the interaction of painting and installation. He has held numerous solo and group exhibitions in Europe and Asia, and his studio is currently located on Cheung Chau Island in Hong Kong.

Robert O'Brien was a technical collaborator and engineer associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is noted for his contributions to the technical design and documentation of E.A.T.'s major collaborative projects, most notably the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. O'Brien contributed to the technical reports and research associated with the pavilion's complex systems, such as its innovative sound-activated laser displays and optical equipment. His work often involved the intersection of engineering and media, including specialized photography and reconnaissance technology. He is cited in technical bibliographies related to the organization's efforts to humanize technology and facilitate direct collaboration between artists and scientists, and his writings on these projects were included in publications like 'Pavilion' (1972), which documented the development of the Osaka project.

Robert O'Brien (1951–1988) was a British conceptual artist who became widely known as one half of the artist duo Fortuyn/O'Brien, formed in 1983 with his partner Irene Fortuyn. Although the duo's most prominent activity occurred in the early to mid-1980s, O'Brien was active as an artist from the early 1970s. The duo was noted for exploring the relationship between art objects and their environments, often using computer-rendered images and innovative structural designs in their sculptures and installations. O'Brien passed away prematurely in 1988, after which Irene Fortuyn continued to work under the collective name for several years in honor of their shared artistic mentality. Their works are held in several major international collections, including the Kröller-Müller Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: John B. Nagle (also known as John Nagle) is a computer scientist and engineer known for his pioneering work in computer graphics and character animation, fields which align with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Active since the late 1960s, Nagle has been involved in the technical development of artistic tools, including the creation of 'Falling Bodies,' a physics simulation plug-in for character animation used by digital artists. He also developed early raster-to-vector converters for personal computers and held research positions at Stanford University and Ford Aerospace. His work from the late 1960s and 1970s is cited in contemporary art history dissertations regarding the evolution of computer graphics, 'embodiment' in art, and the history of experimental art games.

John Nagle was an engineer and superintendent for the National Park Service (NPS) who was involved in technical and artistic collaborations during the construction and early years of the Gateway Arch in St. Louis. In the late 1960s (specifically 1968-1970), he is noted for engaging in 'geodesic' field experiments in collaboration with the prominent sculptor Kenneth Snelson. Snelson was a world-renowned artist whose work combined engineering principles with aesthetic form, often associated with 'tensegrity' and geodesic structures. Nagle's role as a technical expert collaborating with an artist on experimental structural systems fits the description of the interdisciplinary projects supported by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

John Nagle is a physicist who was active in the mid-1960s and 1970s at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and later Carnegie Mellon University. During a symposium for the MIT Laboratory for Nuclear Science (LNS), it was noted that Nagle possessed 'a little bit of the poet and artist' and was specifically asked to use computer technology to 'embellish' a complex 'tree' diagram of scientific knowledge. His use of computer-generated graphics to represent scientific concepts aesthetically represents the overlap between technology and artistic expression characteristic of the E.A.T. era. He and his wife, Stephanie Tristram-Nagle, are also known for their long career in experimental biophysics.
